text: The robotic arms are positioned to assist in the repair of a diaphragmatic hernia, with instruments maneuvering to access and manipulate the hernia site.

Our patient is a sixty-year-old female with a history of alcoholic cirrhosis, child class A, with portal hypertension and variceal disease. She had a known hiatal hernia that had caused her gastroesophageal reflux, which was somewhat refractory to PPI management. More recently, she had developed some substernal epigastric pain, which was often worse after meals. Also in her history, she had had a left mastectomy with a tram flap reconstruction for fibrocystic disease of her left breast.

An upper endoscopy was performed at an outside institution, which demonstrated a seven centimeter hiatal hernia and evidence of a portal hypertension, including portal gastropathy and varices. Her variceal disease was a contraindication for a formal hiatal hernia repair with misinfundal placation.

Due to the patient's comorbidities, we elected for a minimally invasive approach to her hiatal hernia repair. A CT scan of the patient's chest abdomen was obtained, and this demonstrated an anterior substernal hernia with colonic contents. The coronal views demonstrated a narrow neck for this anterior hernia.
Both of these hernias were consistent with the patient's uncontrolled gastroesophageal reflux, as well as her postprandial substernal and epigastric abdominal pain.

The patient is positioned supine, and after entry with an optical trocar, working ports are established and the surgical robot is docked. The surgeon begins by carefully reducing the hernia contents back into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon identifies a large anterior defect in the diaphragm, measuring approximately ten by five centimeters, originating from the falciform ligament. Minor attachments to the hernia sac are dissected free using ultrasonic shears. With the hernia fully reduced, the nodular liver, indicative of cirrhosis, is visible, allowing for a clearer assessment of the hernia defect.

Typically, an ideal repair would involve resection of the hernia sac and closure of the hernia defect with or without mesh. In this particular case, however, the defect was massive and would require a much larger operation to fully dissect. The hernia appeared to originate from the patient's tram flap, which had violated the integrity of her abdominal wall.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a 2-0 long-acting absorbable monofilament suture, employing a locking technique by running the suture from both ends and tying it in the middle. A reinforcing mesh underlay is placed beneath the closed defect.
The surgeon uses a 30-degree camera positioned upwards to view the interior abdominal wall. The articulating arms of the robot are visible, facilitating efficient intercorporeal suturing.

Although not depicted in this recording, the operating console uses the stereoscopic video from the camera to allow for depth perception for the surgeon, another advantage of the platform. Stability of the camera and of the working instruments can also improve ease of use. The major disadvantage of using the robot is a lack of haptic feedback. Tension is not translated into the operating console, and so the operator must focus on visual cues and take additional care when handling delicate structures, such as bowel.

The surgeon uses a flexible measuring stick to measure the hernia defect, which is approximately ten centimeters in length. A ten by fifteen centimeter piece of porcine urinary bladder xenograft mesh is selected and rehydrated to be used as buttress material.

While the mesh is hydrating, the surgeon focuses on the patient's type II paraesophageal hernia, which is visually small. The surgeon performs a reduction of the hernia and secures the stomach with a gastropexy due to the patient's medical comorbidities.

The surgeon introduces the rehydrated mesh into the abdomen, marked with a pen for orientation. An absorbable laparoscopic tacker is used to secure the mesh initially at the center of the defect and then around its periphery. Additional two-oh absorbable monofilament stitches are placed at the corners and edges of the mesh, with tacking stitches in the center. A grid pattern of tacks is applied to ensure contact with the peritoneum and minimize dead space. The previously placed gastropexy sutures are elevated to reduce the stomach into the abdomen, to be tied down later.
An intraoperative abdominal gastrointestinal diagnostic (AGD) procedure is performed, revealing the patient's type II peri-esophageal hernia without any visible Cameron's ulcers.
The surgeon closes the twelve millimeter ports using figure-of-eight ovicral stitches with a Carter-Thompson suture passer. The gastropexy stitches are tied down, completing the procedure.

This video highlights the use of newer technologies such as robot assistance with the xenograft mesh underlay repair. Using this material allowed us to avoid leaving permanent material inside of the patient, which would limit possible foreign body reaction, such as infection or erosion in this high-risk patient.

The surgeon employs a minimal approach to repair the posterior type II paraesophageal hernia, using an anterior gastropexy technique to control symptoms. This approach avoids the need for a laparotomy or formal hiatal hernia repair, thereby reducing the risk associated with the patient's varices.
